title: How to Navigate Psychedelic Therapy When You Are in Recovery from Addiction
body:
How to Move through Psychedelic Therapy When You Are in Recovery from Addiction

You're in recovery. Maybe you’ve been sober for a few months or a few years. You’ve done the work - therapy, meetings, maybe medication. You’re committed to staying clean. But now there’s this interest in psychedelic therapy. You’ve heard stories about how it can help with addiction, anxiety, depression. You’re wondering, “Can I do this? Should I?”

Look, you’re not alone in asking these questions. Psychedelic therapy is gaining traction, but if you’re in recovery, there’s a lot to consider before stepping into that space. This isn’t about jumping in blindly or chasing the “magic cure.” It’s about being thoughtful, cautious, and honest with yourself. Right?!

Why Psychedelic Therapy Might Feel Like a Good Idea

The truth is, addiction recovery can be a long, winding road. You might feel stuck in patterns that therapy and 12-step programs haven’t fully addressed. Psychedelics like psilocybin and MDMA are showing promise in clinical trials for helping people process trauma, reduce cravings, and gain new perspectives on their behavior.

Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link) dives into the science and stories behind this. He shares accounts of people who’ve found freedom from addiction through psychedelic experiences. But he also highlights the risks, especially if you’re not careful about set, setting, and mental health history.

So yeah, it’s tempting. But here’s the thing, you’re not just anyone. You’re a person in recovery. That means your brain and body are still healing from the effects of addiction. And that changes the game.

What You Need to Know Before You Start

  • Talk to your healthcare provider. No exceptions. Whether it’s your therapist, psychiatrist, or doctor, get their input. They can help assess whether psychedelic therapy is safe for you, especially if you’re on medications like antidepressants or anti-cravings drugs.
  • Understand your triggers. Psychedelics can bring up intense emotions and memories. If your addiction was tied to trauma, psychedelics might open the floodgates. That can be healing but also dangerous without proper support.
  • Choose the right setting. Psychedelic therapy isn’t a solo trip. It should be done under professional guidance in a controlled environment. Clinics that specialize in addiction recovery are best.
  • Prepare and integrate. The experience itself is one part. The days, weeks, and months after are where the real work happens. You’ll need to process what came up, sometimes with your therapist or support group.

The Risk of Relapse: What You Can’t Ignore

This is the elephant in the room. Psychedelic therapy isn’t a magic bullet, and there’s no guarantee it won’t trigger cravings or relapse. Honestly, some people find the experience so intense it stirs up old patterns. That’s why careful screening and follow-up are non-negotiable.

Here’s a story to bring it home. Maria was clean for three years before trying psilocybin therapy. She went into it hopeful but unprepared for the emotional storm that came up. Without a strong integration plan, she found herself slipping back into old habits. It wasn’t because psychedelics “made” her relapse. It was because she didn’t have the right support.

Truth is, if you want to explore psychedelics while in recovery, you have to treat it like any other high-risk activity. Be honest about your mental health, your cravings, and your support system.

How to Find a Clinic That’s Right for You

Not all psychedelic therapy clinics are created equal, especially for people in recovery. You want a place with experience treating addiction, not just anxiety or depression. Here’s what to look for:

  • Accreditation and licensing. Make sure the clinic operates legally and follows medical guidelines.
  • Experienced staff. Look for therapists trained in addiction and trauma as well as psychedelic facilitation.
  • Clear protocols. The clinic should have thorough screening, preparation sessions, supervised dosing, and integration follow-ups.
  • Transparency. They should be upfront about risks and what you can expect.

If you’re in the US, a good place to start is the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies (MAPS) website, which lists ongoing clinical trials and vetted centers. Also, check out articles on Nature.com for the latest research on psychedelics and addiction treatment.

Practical Tips to Prepare for Psychedelic Therapy in Recovery

Preparation makes a world of difference. Here are some concrete steps you can take to set yourself up for the safest, most helpful experience:

  • Journal your intentions. Spend at least two weeks writing about why you want to try psychedelic therapy. What do you hope to heal? What scares you? This helps ground your mindset.
  • Build a support network. Tell a trusted friend or sponsor about your plans. Arrange check-ins before and after the session.
  • Practice mindfulness or meditation. These tools help you stay present during intense moments and can be useful for integration afterward.
  • Stay sober before the session. No alcohol, no drugs. Your brain needs to be as clear as possible.
  • Read up. Books like A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link) give personal insights into how psychedelics can shift mood and mindset. It’s not recovery-specific, but a good foundation.

During the Session: What to Expect

Most clinics will have you arrive with a guide or therapist. The room will be safe, comfortable, and quiet. Your guide will check in on how you’re feeling throughout. You won’t be left alone. That matters.

The experience itself can last 4 to 8 hours depending on the substance. You might see vivid visuals, feel waves of emotion, or have insights about your addiction and recovery. Be ready for anything. Some moments are joyful. Others can be painful or confusing. That’s okay.

Remember: you’re not supposed to “fight” the experience. Let it happen. Your therapist will help you work through it afterward.

After the Session: Integration Is Where the Healing Happens

Integration means taking what you learned in the psychedelic session and applying it to your daily life. This might involve talking through your experience with a therapist, journaling, or sharing in a support group.

Recovery from addiction isn’t linear. Psychedelic therapy might give you a new perspective, but it won’t erase the work you’ve done or the challenges ahead. It can open a door, but you still have to walk through it.

If you want practical tools for integration, check out my article on Integration After Psychedelic Therapy. It’s full of concrete steps you can take to make sense of your session without getting overwhelmed.

Can You Self-Microdose While in Recovery?

Microdosing has become popular for mood and focus. But in addiction recovery, it’s a tricky subject. Small doses of psychedelics might seem harmless, but they can trigger cravings or destabilize your mental state.

If you’re curious, talk first with your healthcare provider. If you do try it, keep a detailed log of your doses, effects, and any changes in cravings or mood. Remember, microdosing isn’t therapy - it’s an experimental tool. Be cautious.

And if you want to experiment safely at home, consider a mushroom growing kit (paid link) that lets you control potency and dosage carefully. But again, only if you’ve got the green light from your doctor.

Keep Learning: Knowledge Is Your Best Ally

Look, addiction recovery is hard. Adding psychedelics to the mix can feel like stepping into unknown territory. So keep educating yourself. Read books, listen to podcasts, connect with experienced guides.

Merlin Sheldrake’s Entangled Life by Merlin Sheldrake (paid link) isn’t about addiction or therapy specifically, but it gives a fascinating look at fungi and their connections to life - and that can deepen your respect for what you’re working with.

And of course, stay connected with communities that understand both recovery and psychedelics. You can find support and lessons at places like kalesh.love, where I share resources and guidance.

Final Thoughts

There’s no one-size-fits-all answer here. Psychedelic therapy can offer powerful healing, but only if you approach it with caution, respect, and a clear plan. If you’re in recovery, your priority has to be safety and long-term wellness. No shortcuts.

Does that land? It’s about balancing hope with realism. Being brave without being reckless. And always, always having support around you.

If you want to learn more about psychedelic therapy and how it intersects with mental health, check out the resources on NCBI for peer-reviewed studies and clinical guidelines.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
